actions.thought: I am on the Button in a 3-handed game, meaning I am first to act pre-flop. My hand is 54o (Five of Clubs, Four of Spades), which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. It has extremely poor playability and very low equity against any standard continuing range. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

With the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. King-high is a decent holding in a blind-vs-blind scenario. By raising to 250, I put pressure on the big blind to either fold or commit more chips, allowing me to take down the pot immediately or play the hand with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 250. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.3 to 1 to call, Ten-Deuce is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given it is the first hand of the tournament and I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold this marginal hand.

The cards 9d 4h are weak, uncoordinated, and have very low equity against the ranges of the two opponents. Even though I am on the button, playing this hand from the start is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for stronger hands in this 3-handed tournament format.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While blind-vs-blind situations allow for a wider range, J4o is too weak to play profitably out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 5h 2h. While these cards are suited, they are extremely weak in terms of high card value and connectivity. In a 3-handed game, I should play a wide range from the button, but 52s is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise or call. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

73 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Even though I only have to call 50 more to complete the small blind, playing this trash hand out of position against the Big Blind is a long-term losing play. Folding is the correct decision.
T3o is a very weak offsuit hand. Being first to act on the button (since the blinds are yet to act), it is not worth a raise or a call. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, 5h-Td. Playing out of position against the big blind with a marginal hand this early in the tournament is not optimal. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

The hand 2s 9c is extremely weak and lacks any significant playability. Even with the positional advantage of the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into this hand. Folding is the clear, disciplined choice.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 5c. Flashlight on the button has folded. It is just me and the Big Blind (Pro). While 3c 5c is suited, it is quite weak to play out of position against a player labeled 'Pro'. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal hand that is difficult to play post-flop.

Holding Jh 7h on the Button in a 3-handed game is a strong candidate for an opening raise. Suited hands with decent connectivity have good playability and realize their equity well. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot.

We hold 5d4d in the small blind facing a 3x open from the button. Calling here is risky due to being out of position post-flop and the big blind potentially squeezing. Suited connectors like 54s play best when we have position or fold equity. While it could occasionally be a 3-bet bluff, the most solid and standard play with 100BB stacks is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c, Th. While I have the pot odds to call a 300 raise (costing 200 into a pot of 450), my hand is very weak and lacks high card strength or connectivity. Playing a weak hand out of position post-flop is a recipe for losing chips. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Pocket fours is a respectable hand in a 3-handed game. As the first to act on the Button, an open-raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard play. It puts pressure on the blinds to fold their weaker hands, allows us to steal the pot pre-flop, and disguises our hand if we manage to flop a set.

I hold Ace-Five offsuit in the small blind. Given the button's open-raise, this hand has decent enough high-card strength and board coverage to defend. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, flat-calling is a standard approach here to keep the pot manageable rather than bloating it with a marginal hand against a potentially strong range.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six suited. The Button raised to 2.5x and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more into a 600 pot, giving me excellent 5:1 pot odds. J6s plays reasonably well multi-way and I am closing the pre-flop action. Calling is the most profitable play here to see a flop.

The board texture of King-Ten-Nine is quite connected and likely favors the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) or the Big Blind who can hold various straight draws and two-pair combinations. With Ace-Five high, I have completely missed the board. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the action from my opponents, as betting would be a pure bluff into two people on a board that hits most ranges better than mine.

I have a gutshot straight draw to a Queen on a highly coordinated board (9-T-K). Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the Small Blind has already checked, I will check as well. This allows me to see how the Button reacts and potentially see a turn card cheaply or for free.

We hold pocket fours on a very wet and high-card heavy board (9c Td Ks). This texture connects heavily with the calling ranges of both the Small Blind and Big Blind, making it very likely one of them hit a piece of this flop (a pair, a straight draw, etc.). A continuation bet here into two opponents is unlikely to succeed as a bluff. Taking a free card by checking behind is the best approach to try and hit a miracle set on the turn or simply keep the pot small.

The turn has paired my five, giving me bottom pair. While this is an improvement, the board remains highly connected with multiple straight and flush possibilities. Given that all three players checked the flop, the betting interest is low, and my hand is not strong enough to bet for value. Checking allows me to keep the pot size under control and reach a potential showdown for free, as betting would likely only isolate me against stronger hands or force out hands that are already worse than mine.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs improves my hand significantly, giving me a flush draw to go along with my gutshot straight draw (any Queen). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked the flop, indicating weakness or a pot-controlling medium-strength hand. The Small Blind has also checked twice. Since I have significant equity (roughly 11 outs) but only Jack-high currently, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see the river for free if Pro checks again, or evaluate a call if a bet is made, maintaining pot control while drawing to a very strong hand.

Both opponents have checked again on the turn. Our pocket fours are still very weak on this board, and betting would be a pure bluff against two opponents. The board has flush and straight draws, meaning they might still call with a wide range of hands. Checking behind is the safest play to keep the pot small, realize our limited equity, and see a free river card.

I hold a pair of fives, giving me two pair (tens and fives) on this board of 9-T-K-5-T. However, the board is highly coordinated, and any ten gives an opponent trips, while any king gives them a better two pair. Given that the action has been checked through on all streets, my showdown value is marginal. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (such as a ten or king) or might fold out hands that I am currently beating. Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown without inflating the pot unnecessarily.

The board has paired on the river (9c-Td-Ks-5c-Th) and all players, including the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), have checked on every street so far (Flop and Turn). I have Jack-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, the action (or lack thereof) suggests that nobody has a particularly strong hand. Flashlight has checked to me on the river. While a bluff might occasionally work here given the passive nature of the hand, I am out of position against the Button and there are two opponents to push off their hands. The most prudent play in this early stage of the tournament is to check and take the free showdown, or fold if a significant bet comes from the Button, rather than risking chips on a low-probability bluff.

The board has paired Tens on the river, giving us two pair (Tens and Fours) with a King kicker. Since both opponents checked through the flop, turn, and river, their ranges are likely heavily weighted towards busted straight/flush draws or Ace-high. A bet here would only get called by better hands (like a 9, a K, or a higher pocket pair) while folding out all the missed draws that we currently beat. Checking behind allows us to safely take our hand to showdown and realize our showdown value.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wide opening range. Despite holding Jack-Seven offsuit, which is a marginal hand, it is a standard strategic play to raise and attempt to steal the blinds, leveraging my position advantage for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 250. While button ranges are typically wide, Q6o is too weak to defend out of position, especially with a solid player in the Big Blind still to act. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.
9c 5h is a very weak hand, unsuited and disconnected. Facing a raise from the Button, folding from the Big Blind is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with 6-4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While button aggression is important, 6-4o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call against the blinds, especially early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 3d, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Being out of position against the Big Blind with a hand that has very poor equity and no playability, the standard and correct play is to fold.
I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with 96o. This is a very weak starting hand, not strong enough to open-raise or call even from late position. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

7-10 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially from the Small Blind out of position. With no initiative and poor connectivity, there is no strategic value in completing or raising. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

KQo is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game, especially when in position on the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a post-flop hand with the lead. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective opening size.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce of Hearts. Flashlight on the Button has opened with a 3x raise to 300. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak (J2s) and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand if I call. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act and could potentially squeeze. Given the stack depths and the poor playability of J2s against a button raising range, folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play to preserve chips for better opportunities.
Holding 8-4 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is a very weak, unplayable hand with no connectivity or high card value. Folding is the only optimal play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x open-raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I hold 6s 4d offsuit in the Small Blind, facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is a very weak hand with no high card value, suitedness, or strong connectivity. The standard and optimal play is to fold and save chips for better opportunities.

Ten-five offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity, especially out of position against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify a call, and continuing with such a weak hand would likely lead to a difficult and unprofitable post-flop situation. Folding is the standard, disciplined play here to preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with 73o, which is a very weak starting hand. The standard play is to fold pre-flop to avoid entering a pot with a significant hand disadvantage.

With the button folding, I am heads-up in the small blind against the big blind. King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in this spot, granting me a significant range advantage. Raising is the optimal strategy to capitalize on this advantage, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds immediately. I will raise to 300, which is a standard sizing to apply pressure in this blind-versus-blind dynamic.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Seven suited. The Small Blind has raised to 300 (a 3x open). In a blind-vs-blind scenario, the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide. Jack-Seven suited is a strong enough hand to defend against this range, especially given that I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to win a pot that will be 600), which makes calling the most profitable and standard play here.

The board of 9-2-8 is very dry and low, missing my K-high holding. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I still hold the range advantage. I will make a small continuation bet to test the opponent and potentially take down the pot immediately, as it is likely they also missed this texture. If called, I will re-evaluate based on the turn card.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10 for a J-high straight) and one overcard with my Jack. The board of 9s-2h-8d is relatively dry, and Flashlight's lead of 200 into 600 (a 1/3 pot bet) is a standard continuation bet from the Small Blind. I am receiving 4:1 pot odds (200 to call into a pot that will be 1000), which easily justifies a call with my current equity and the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand.

The board has paired on the turn with the 8c, which significantly strengthens any range that includes an 8, and my opponent's call on the flop suggests they have at least a piece of this dry board. My King-high is weak and essentially a bluff at this point. Betting here would likely yield little fold equity against a calling range that has already demonstrated interest. Checking is the optimal play to control the pot size, avoid bloating it with a marginal hand, and re-evaluate on the river.

Flashlight checked on the turn after a small flop continuation bet. With my gutshot straight draw and Jack-high, checking back is a safe and effective play. It allows me to see the river for free, potentially hitting my straight or a Jack, while avoiding the risk of being check-raised if I were to bluff. This maximizes my chances of realizing my hand's equity in position.

The board runout (9-2-8-8-5) is extremely dry, and I am holding King-Jack high, which has zero showdown value against any made hand, including a pair of deuces, fives, or nines. Since the turn was checked through, my opponent likely has some marginal showdown value that they would not fold to a bet, or they have a hand that beat me that they were trapping with. A bet here would essentially be a total bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any part of the opponent's continuing range. Therefore, checking behind is the most sensible play to minimize losses.

The board has run out 9s-2h-8d-8c-5s and Flashlight has checked for the second street in a row after a small continuation bet on the flop. This line usually indicates a weak hand or a 'give up'. However, my Jack-high has almost no showdown value against his range, which likely includes Ace-highs or small pocket pairs that might check-call a small river bet. While a bluff could be effective here given his perceived weakness, I choose to check back and realize what little equity I have rather than risk a significant portion of my stack on a pure bluff this early in the tournament.

King-Four suited is a weak hand, even on the button in a three-handed game. With over 100 big blinds, there is no pressure to play marginal hands, and it is more strategic to wait for a stronger opening hand. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

Action is on me in the Small Blind after the Button folded. Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o) is a very weak hand. While it's only 50 chips to call, Q2o plays poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against Pro. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, it is more prudent to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity.

KJo is a strong premium hand in a 3-handed game, especially from the button. I want to raise for value and to seize the initiative. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate given the previous hand histories and stack sizes.
I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the most profitable and standard play is to fold.

I hold pocket deuces in the Big Blind. This is a classic set-mining scenario. The pot odds are favorable to call a 3x raise from the button, as I only need to contribute 200 more chips to a 450 pot. Pocket pairs play well by flopping sets, and with a deep stack of 11,100, I have the implied odds to make this call profitable. Raising here would likely inflate the pot with a marginal hand that performs poorly if called and missed, so a simple call is the most strategic approach.

I flopped bottom set of deuces on a board with two clubs. As the pre-flop caller and the player out of position, checking is the optimal strategy. This allows the pre-flop aggressor to potentially fire a continuation bet with their air or weaker pairs, which I can then capitalize on by check-raising or just calling to keep them bluffing on later streets. Leading out would likely fold out the hands I want to keep in the pot.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding two overcards (King and Jack) on a relatively dry board. Flashlight has checked to me. A continuation bet is standard here to capitalize on my range advantage and fold out hands like small pairs or random high cards that missed. A bet of around 40% of the pot should be sufficient to test their strength while keeping the pot manageable if they call.

I hold the bottom set on a board with a flush draw. With the pre-flop aggressor leading out, this is the perfect opportunity to check-raise. Raising serves two purposes: it builds a larger pot for my strong hand and puts pressure on any draws the opponent might have, forcing them to pay a premium to see the next card. A raise to 750 is a standard size to commit more value to the pot while keeping my range disguised.

After my continuation bet on the flop, Flashlight has check-raised me. This board (9s-7c-2c) is relatively dry, which makes a check-raise look very strong, likely representing a set, two pair, or a high-equity flush draw. My King-high has some equity with two overcards, but I lack any backdoors or clubs to justify calling a raise. I am likely behind, and calling would put me in a difficult spot on the turn without the initiative. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.
J2s is a very weak hand. Even though I have the button, it is not worth a raise or a call. Folding is the optimal play here.

With the action folded to me in the small blind, I have a prime opportunity to steal the blinds. King-high is a strong enough hand to open with a raise to 300, putting pressure on the big blind to either fold or commit more chips. This is a standard aggressive move in this position.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 5d. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. Although I will have position post-flop, Q5o is a weak hand that does not play well in raised pots. Flashlight has been active, but defending with such a marginal hand when I am currently middle of the pack in chips is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

My starting hand, 3d 6c, is extremely weak and lacks any significant equity or playability. Even in a shorthanded game on the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips with such a poor holding. I will fold to conserve my stack for more profitable situations.

With the Button folding, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. J4s is a marginal hand but strong enough to attempt a steal from the Small Blind, especially since Pro (Big Blind) has folded to several pre-flop raises in earlier hands. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) exerts pressure and allows me to take down the pot immediately or play with initiative if called.

Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, 4h 5d (offsuit low connector) is simply too weak to defend, even though we will have position post-flop. The hand has poor playability and very low equity against a typical raising range. Folding to preserve the stack is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard play to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I have position on both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Flashlight) for the rest of the hand if they choose to call.
Q2o is a very weak starting hand, and facing a raise from the button while out of position in the small blind makes it an easy fold. I will save my chips for a better opportunity.

I have Ace-Eight suited in the Big Blind, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a button open. Given Flash's aggressive style, calling is the standard and most profitable approach here to keep the pot manageable while maintaining equity against their wider opening range.

I am in the Big Blind and first to act on a flop of 9s 3h 4s. I hold Ah 8h. My hand is currently just Ace-high, which has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the button and evaluate their action.

On the button, I raised pre-flop with Ace-Seven offsuit and Flashlight (Big Blind) called. The flop is 9s 3h 4s. Flashlight has checked to me. While I haven't hit the board directly, I still have two overcards and the best hand sometimes. This board is relatively dry, although there is a spade draw. A continuation bet here is standard to try and take the pot down immediately, representing a strong range that includes high pairs or a 9. I will bet around half the pot to maintain pressure.

The opponent has made a standard continuation bet on a low, connected board (9s 3h 4s). My hand, Ace-Eight suited, has reasonable showdown value, an overcard, and backdoor flush potential. While I am out of position, folding to a half-pot bet with an Ace-high hand on such a board seems premature. Calling is the most balanced approach, allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn if the situation changes.

I am out of position on the turn on a board of 9s-3h-4s-4c. My hand, Ace-Eight high, has very little showdown value, and the paired board makes it more likely that the opponent has connected with the board. Betting here would be an ineffective bluff, and I have no reason to lead. Checking is the standard play in this position to either induce a check-back or to see the river while minimizing my investment.

The turn 4c pairs the bottom of the flop. Flashlight has checked for the second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While I have Ace-high, which holds some showdown value against draws, a second barrel here is unlikely to fold out a Nine or a pocket pair that called the flop. By checking back, I control the pot size and get to see a free river card, which could improve my hand to the best pair or allow me to win at showdown if Flashlight is on a missed draw.

The board is 9s-3h-4s-4c-3s, and I hold Ah-8h. My hand has effectively zero showdown value, as even a pocket pair or a weak card like a five or six has me beat. The opponent checked the turn, suggesting they do not have a strong hand themselves or are pot-controlling. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that is likely to have at least a pair, given the double-paired board. Checking allows me to reach showdown if they also check, and I gain nothing by betting. Checking is the only logical play.

On this board of 9s-3h-4s-4c-3s, the community cards have paired twice (Fours and Threes). My best five-card hand is two pair, Fours and Threes, with an Ace kicker (4-4-3-3-A). Flashlight has checked for the third time this hand (flop call, turn check, river check). While I don't have a Nine or a pocket pair, my Ace kicker is the best possible kicker for the two pair on the board. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a Nine or a pocket pair, and I have significant showdown value against missed draws or lower kickers. I will check back to take the hand to showdown and potentially win or chop the pot.
I am holding an extremely weak offsuit hand (3h 6d) and I am first to act pre-flop on the Button. The only correct play is to fold and preserve my chips for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with King-Nine suited. With the button folding, I am in a heads-up pot against the big blind. Raising here is the standard, aggressive play to take down the blinds or gain the initiative, as K9s is a strong enough hand to play aggressively from the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands, and defending with such a marginal hand OOP often leads to difficult post-flop decisions where equity realization is low. I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for better opportunities.

Holding 4-2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with no equity, connectivity, or showdown value. There is no strategic justification for investing chips into the pot with this holding, even on the button. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack for higher-value opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 4h. It has been folded to me. J4 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal with a raise, the hand has poor playability if called. Completing the small blind (calling) also puts me in a difficult spot post-flop. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative, consistent with my previous opening sizes.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button, T8s is a marginal suited one-gapper. Playing it from the Small Blind means being out of position post-flop, and we still have the Big Blind left to act behind us, risking a squeeze. Folding is the safest and most standard option here to avoid difficult out-of-position spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 5c, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), 2-5 offsuit has terrible equity realization and will be difficult to play post-flop, especially out of position. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am dealt 3s 2c offsuit, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being the first to act pre-flop on the button, the most straightforward and optimal play is to fold to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with 9d8d, a suited connector with decent playability. The button folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Raising from the small blind is a standard strategy to steal the blinds or take the initiative in the hand, especially with suited cards. I will raise to 250 to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o). The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 2.5x. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call, T5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I've been folding frequently, I don't see a reason to defend with such a low-equity hand here. I will wait for a better spot.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak, non-connected hand that lacks the equity to play profitably even from the button. In a three-handed game, the button acts first pre-flop, making it essentially the 'under the gun' position. Opening with such a weak hand would be undisciplined and likely to lead to difficult post-flop scenarios. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Tc4c. The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). Looking at the history of the game, Pro has consistently folded their Big Blind to 3x raises (300 chips). Since the hand Tc4c has some potential but is weak, and Pro is playing very tight in the blinds, a raise to 300 is a high-frequency profitable play here as a steal attempt.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, 67o is generally too weak to defend, even with position. While we do have some playability, an offsuit connector like this will struggle to realize its equity against a strong opening range and the sizing reduces our pot odds. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit. While I have been active in recent hands, this hand is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain stack integrity and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if called.
With 4d 5c offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to play, especially out of position against the big blind. Folding is the most standard and solid choice.
With 9d 4d on the Button, this hand is too weak to attempt a pre-flop steal against the blinds. It has poor equity and playability post-flop, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I hold a strong pocket pair, Tens, in the small blind with the button having folded. This is a prime opportunity to raise and put pressure on the big blind to either fold or play a bloated pot, where my hand has significant equity. I will raise to 300 to assert control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 600 total. This gives me 2:1 pot odds, requiring approximately 33% equity to break even. Against a typical aggressive Small Blind opening range, King-Six offsuit usually holds enough equity, especially since I will have position for the remainder of the hand. While the hand is marginal, folding would be too tight given the price and position, and raising is unnecessary given the hand's strength. I will call to see the flop.

The board features an Ace, which is highly likely to have connected with the opponent's big blind calling range. With a pair of Tens, I am currently behind any Ace and vulnerable to better pairs. Since I am out of position, checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action, rather than bloat a pot against a range that likely has me beat.

On this flop of 7c-6c-Ad, I have middle pair (sixes). Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. This check often indicates either a marginal hand looking for pot control, a complete whiff, or occasionally a trap with a strong Ace. Since I have showdown value with my pair and want to keep the pot small while in position, checking back is the most prudent play. Betting risks getting check-raised off my equity or value-cutting myself against an Ace or a Seven. By checking, I control the pot and can see how the turn develops.

The board has become much more dangerous with the completion of a potential club flush on the turn. Holding Tens without a club, my hand is extremely vulnerable to any flush, as well as any Ace, which is well within the opponent's calling range. Checking is the most strategic play here to control the pot size and avoid putting more chips into a likely losing hand. I will check to see the river as cheaply as possible.

I hold middle pair (sixes) and the second-best possible flush draw with the King of clubs. The board currently shows three clubs and an Ace. I am on the button with 9s-7s on a turn board of 6c-4s-5h-6s. This is a standard semi-bluff strategy, taking the free card to improve my hand or reassess on the river.

The river double pairs the board (6s and 4s), meaning my pocket Aces give me Aces and Sixes, which is an extremely strong hand here. Therefore, betting is the best option to extract value from hands like 88-JJ that might look you up, suspecting you have a missed AK or AQ. A sizing of roughly 1/3 to 1/2 pot should be enticing enough for his bluff-catchers to call.

I am holding 9s-7s on a board of 6c-4s-5h-6s-4h. My best five-card hand is 6-6-4-4-9. Opponent Pro's 3-bet pre-flop range is strong (77+, AJs+, KQs, etc.). Since 'Pro' checked both the flop and the turn, they might be weak, but they could also be trapping. Given that I only have a marginal improvement over the board and am out of position, checking to see 'Pro's' action is the most disciplined play to reach a showdown cheaply or evaluate a potential bet.

The river brings another Ace, making the board As Qs Ks Qc Ac. The board itself plays as Two Pair (Aces and Queens) with a King kicker. Holding 9d Td, I am simply playing the board. I hold a pair of Tens on a board showing 4d-Th-Kc-8s. My hand is of marginal strength and does not necessarily benefit from aggressive betting, as better hands like a King or a pair of Jacks/Queens would likely call or raise, while worse hands that I have already beaten will fold. Checking allows me to keep the pot small, control the betting, and evaluate the opponent's action on the river before deciding whether to call or fold.

In this limped pot, the board of King-high with a Ten and an Eight offers no connectivity to my 7-high. Flashlight has checked for the second time, indicating weakness. However, with no pair and no draw, betting into a dry board in a small pot is unnecessary. I will check back to see the river for free and avoid risking chips with zero equity.

I hold a pair of Tens on a board of 4-T-K-8-A. The presence of an Ace and a King on the board significantly diminishes the value of my Tens, as any opponent holding an Ace or King has me beaten. Given the pot is small (200) and the previous streets have been checked down by both players, there is no reason to lead out with a bet. Betting would only likely result in being called by better hands while forcing out hands I might already beat, such as total bluffs. Checking is the most prudent strategy to reach a cheap showdown and minimize any further loss.

In this limped pot, the board has run out with several high cards (King, Ten, and now an Ace). Flashlight has checked on every single street, indicating a very weak range, likely consisting of low pairs or missed high-card draws. However, my 7-high has zero showdown value. In a tournament setting with my current chip lead, there is little incentive to risk chips on a pure bluff in such a marginal spot. I will check it down and concede the pot if Flashlight has any pair or a better high card.

My hand, 2d 6s, is a very weak, uncoordinated holding with no potential to connect strongly with the board. In a short-handed tournament setting with 3 players, preserving my stack for better spots is the priority. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six of hearts (Ah 6h), which is a very strong hand for a Blind vs Blind scenario after the Button has folded. Against 'Pro' in the Big Blind, I want to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has both high card value and flush potential. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either take the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if called (or out of position but with the lead).

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand. Facing a Small Blind open, I should 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. Since I have position post-flop, a 3-bet to 3x (900 chips) is ideal to extract value from worse pairs and broadway hands while denying equity.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Ace-Six suited is quite strong. Pro has 3-bet my initial raise to 900. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the pot odds for a call are reasonable (600 to win a total pot of 1800), and my hand has good playability and nut-flush potential. I will call the 3-bet to see a flop rather than 4-betting and potentially facing a shove from Pro.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board (T-Q-A). Since 'Pro' was the pre-flop 3-better, I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. This board hits a 3-betting range very hard, and I need to be cautious about being out-kicked or facing a made straight or set.

The flop comes Th Qd Ad, which is terrible for pocket Jacks as it consists entirely of overcards and coordinates heavily with a 3-bet calling range. While I have a gutshot and the Jd blocker, betting here achieves little value and risks a check-raise or a call from hands that crush me (any Ace, Queen, or stronger draws). Checking behind for pot control and to realize my equity is the optimal play.

The turn card is a Jack of clubs, making the board Ten-Queen-Ace-Jack. This board is extremely coordinated, with any King now holding a straight. Since 'Pro' 3-bet pre-flop and then checked the flop, they could be trapping with a monster or holding a hand like AK or KK that has now improved or maintains high value. My Ace-Six is now just top pair with a very weak kicker on a board where many hands in a 3-betting range (AK, KQ, KJs, sets) beat me. Checking again is the safest play to control the pot size and see how 'Pro' reacts to the four-to-a-straight texture.

The turn brings a Jack, giving me a set, but it also puts a four-card straight to a King on the board (T-J-Q-A). Betting here has very little value as I'll mostly be called by hands that contain a King, which currently beat me. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity to potentially hit a full house or quads on the river, and avoid getting check-raised by the nuts.

The river pairs the Tens on an already highly coordinated board (A-Q-J-T-T). My Ace-Six provides two pair, Aces and Tens, with a Queen kicker. However, I am potentially beaten by a wide variety of hands in 'Pro's' 3-betting range, including any King (straight), any Ten (trips), or pocket pairs like QQ and JJ which have now made full houses. Given the passive play on the flop and turn, checking is the safest way to reach showdown or evaluate a potential bet from 'Pro', as I have no value in betting myself.

The river pairs the board with a Ten, giving me a full house, Jacks full of Tens. My opponent has checked down all three post-flop streets, making it highly unlikely they hold a stronger full house like Aces full, Queens full, or quads. They most likely have a straight (any King) or a strong top-pair type hand like AQ that was scared of the highly coordinated and dangerous board. I have a clear value bet here. Betting around 1/3 of the pot is the optimal sizing to extract value from these weaker hands, as a larger bet might scare off the exact hands I want to get called by.

On a board of Ad-Qd-Th-Jc-Tc, my Ah-6h constitutes two pair, Aces and Tens, with a Queen kicker. While the board is extremely dangerous with four cards to a Broadway straight (any King) and a paired Ten (any Ten or full house), the betting action suggests a call. Pro 3-bet pre-flop but then checked both the flop and the turn. This passivity makes it less likely they are holding a monster like a full house or a straight, though they could be trapping. On the river, Pro's bet of 600 into a 2400 pot is small (1/4 pot), providing me with 4-to-1 pot odds. I lose to any King, any Ten, or AQ/AJ, but I split the pot with any other weak Ace (A9-A2) because the Queen on the board acts as the fifth card for both of us. Given the odds and the possibility of a split or a bluff, calling is the most mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button with pocket deuces (2d, 2s). In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong opening hand from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a favorable post-flop situation if either opponent calls.
Q7 offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind facing a button open. Out of position and with poor playability, folding is the standard and correct play.

I hold Ace-Five suited, which is a strong hand to defend against a button open from a reasonably aggressive player like Flash. With the current pot odds and my position, calling is the most balanced approach. A three-bet could lead to a difficult situation if Flash shoves, so I will take a flop to realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with top pair (Ace of Spades, 5 of Spades) on a dry board of Ace-high, 9-high, 3-high. As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor (Button), the standard and most strategic play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to control the pot size and observe Flash's reaction to the board.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace on the flop heavily favors my perceived range over the Big Blind's calling range. Flashlight's check allows me to follow through with a continuation bet. A bet of 300 into the 650 pot puts pressure on his non-pair hands and draws while allowing me to take down the pot immediately. Given that pocket deuces have very little showdown value on this board, I want to end the hand now.

I hold top pair on an Ace-high board. While my kicker is weak, folding to a single c-bet is too conservative. The board is dry, so calling keeps Flash's range wide, allowing me to see the turn without inflating the pot unnecessarily. Raising would likely only get called by better hands, which is not ideal.

I hold two pair (Aces and Fives), which is a strong hand, but the board has completed a potential flush with the Three of Clubs, Ace of Clubs, and Five of Clubs. Since I am out of position against an aggressive button player, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to gauge Flash's interest in the pot. If Flash checks behind, I get to see a free river and potentially value-bet; if Flash bets, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if my two pair is still good or if I am beat by a made flush.

The turn 5 of clubs is a significant card as it completes the club flush draw that was present on the flop. After Flashlight check-called my continuation bet on the flop, his range likely includes an Ace, a 9, or a flush draw. Since he checked again on the turn, he is either trapping with a completed flush or holding a marginal made hand like an Ace or a 9. My pocket deuces have almost no showdown value, but they also have no equity to improve. Betting again into a board that just completed a draw is risky, as I am unlikely to fold out an Ace or a flush. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and can decide whether to fold or attempt a cheap showdown depending on the river action.

I have two pair (Aces and Fives) on a board that has completed a potential club flush (3c, Ac, 5c, 8c). My opponent checked back the turn, which signals they likely have a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I have showdown value, my hand is vulnerable to any club or higher two-pair combinations. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands, and checking allows me to safely reach showdown without inflating the pot against a potential flush. Therefore, I will check.

The board has completed a four-card flush (3c, Ac, 5c, 8c). My pocket deuces have no showdown value, as they are beaten by any Ace, nine, eight, five, three, or any single club in my opponent's hand. Flashlight's line (check-calling the flop and checking the turn and river) suggests a marginal made hand or a draw. While I could attempt to bluff by representing a flush, the likelihood that Flashlight holds a single club is high given the board texture. If he has any club, he will call. Given my strong stack position and the risk of being called by even a weak flush or a stubborn Ace, checking back is the safest way to conclude the hand.
I am dealt 9h 3c on the Button, which is a very weak offsuit hand. The correct play in this position with this holding is simply to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand (4-6 offsuit). The Button (Pro) has folded, so I am facing the Big Blind (Flash). While the pot odds are tempting, playing a hand as weak as 4-6o out of position is generally poor strategy, as I will likely struggle on almost any flop. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-Ten suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Since I act first, raising to 300 is a standard opening move to take the initiative and exert pressure on the blinds. This hand plays well post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind holding QJo, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, which is a standard opening. While I could 3-bet to take the initiative, QJo plays well enough in a flatting range, and calling allows me to see a flop without bloating the pot out of position against an aggressive opponent. I am getting decent odds to call (250 to win a pot of 450), and I want to keep the pot manageable since I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have a small pocket pair (3h 3d) in the Big Blind. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called, giving me excellent pot odds (200 to win 700) and deep implied odds to set-mine against two opponents. I will call and look to hit a set on the flop; otherwise, I can easily check-fold.

On this dry, low board (8d 4h 2d), my QJ high has some showdown value but is unlikely to be the best hand if there is significant action. Being out of position against both the pre-flop aggressor and the Big Blind, checking is the most standard and cautious play. This allows me to see how the original raiser (Flashlight) reacts to the flop before deciding whether to continue with my overcards.

I called pre-flop strictly to set-mine with my pocket threes. The flop came 8-4-2, missing my set, leaving me with a very weak pair between the 4 and the 2. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and safest play is to check and prepare to fold to any aggression, as my hand has very little showdown value and poor equity against the ranges of two opponents.

I am in position (button) and raised pre-flop. The flop of 8d-4h-2d is relatively dry and missed my range, but since everyone checked to me, a continuation bet is standard to represent the strength of my pre-flop raise and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a bluff. My King-high has some backdoor equity with the spade draw.

I am facing a 300-chip bet from Flashlight on a very dry board (8d 4h 2d) in a three-way pot. My hand, QJ high, has missed the flop completely and possesses no backdoor draws. While a 1/3 pot bet might sometimes invite a float in a heads-up situation, doing so out of position with another player (Pro) still left to act is significantly riskier. In a multi-way scenario, the pre-flop aggressor's continuation bet usually represents a stronger range. Without a pair or a draw, and with the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I called pre-flop to set-mine with my pocket threes and completely missed the flop. Although I have a pair, it is an extremely weak underpair to the 8 and 4. Now facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser after the Small Blind folded, I am out of position with very little equity or improvement potential against most of their betting range. Following my initial plan, an easy fold is the correct and most disciplined play.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While the button is a prime position to steal the blinds in a three-handed game, Jack-Four is far too weak to open raise or call with. Both opponents have healthy stacks and have shown a willingness to play back or call. There is no strategic reason to commit chips with such a sub-optimal hand at this stage of the tournament. Folding is the standard professional play here.

A5s is a premium starting hand blind-versus-blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind, allows me to win the pot pre-flop frequently, and gives me the initiative going to the flop with a very playable hand that can flop top pair, flush draws, or wheel straight draws.

I am in the Big Blind with QsTs, facing a raise from the Small Blind (Pro). This is a standard defense. With decent suited connected cards, I have good equity, and the pot odds (200 to call in a 400 pot) make calling the correct decision. I will call to see the flop.

The flop of Td Qc 7d heavily favors the Big Blind caller's range, which contains many broadways, suited connectors, and diamond flush draws. I have A5 of spades, giving me no direct draws and only Ace-high. I have top two pair (Queens and Tens) on a somewhat connected, rainbow board. Pro (Small Blind) checked to me, which is a sign of weakness or a potential trap. However, I must bet for value to protect against any potential draws (like diamonds) and to get value from weaker hands like a Ten, a 7, or middle pairs. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard to build the pot and gain information.

After checking to evaluate, Flashlight bet half-pot into a board that connects well with his big blind calling range. With only Ace-high, no direct draws, and no backdoor equity, my hand is too weak to continue. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

K8o is a weak offsuit hand, even on the button. While stealing is an option, the weak kicker makes it vulnerable to being dominated if called or 3-bet. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

Holding 2-5 offsuit in the Small Blind is a very weak hand with poor potential for post-flop equity, especially when playing out of position against the Big Blind. Given the weak starting cards, it is best to fold and preserve my stack rather than investing further chips into a disadvantageous situation.

I am in the button position with 6-8 offsuit, which is a weak hand with poor playability, especially against two opponents. The cost to call is 100 chips into a 150-chip pot. Given the strength of the hand and the position, it is best to fold and conserve my stack.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, J9o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to put pressure on the Big Blind. Raising to 300 (3bb) is a standard opening size to take down the blinds or play the hand with initiative if called.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) on a semi-coordinated board of 9-T-7. As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind battle, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain initiative and extract value from worse hands or draws. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced size that allows me to define where I stand against the Big Blind.

I hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board (9-T-7) that connects heavily with the pre-flop raiser's range. Given the lack of straight or flush draws to improve my hand, and facing a half-pot continuation bet, my equity is marginal at best. Continuing here would likely lead to tough decisions on later streets, so folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving move.

Holding King-Ten offsuit (KTo) on the button in a three-handed game is a strong starting hand. I have a stack of over 100 big blinds and am in the best position. Standard play here is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position. Based on my previous betting patterns, a raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a consistent and effective sizing.

T9 offsuit is too weak to defend from the Small Blind against a button open, especially since we will be out of position for the rest of the hand and the Big Blind is still left to act. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve chips.

I am in the big blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. With the button raising to 300, defending with this hand has poor expected value and very low playability. Folding is the standard and correct play.
J4o is a very weak offsuit hand. Being first to act on the button, standard play is to fold rather than attempt a marginal steal, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

With the button folding, I am now heads-up against the big blind. Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the small blind, I have a strong hand that dominates many of the big blind's potential range. Raising to 200 is a standard move to take control of the pot and apply pressure, rather than just calling and seeing a cheap flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Four offsuit. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has min-raised to 200. I have the chip lead and am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop. Ace-Four is well ahead of a typical Small Blind stealing range, but it's not strong enough to warrant a 3-bet for value given the stack depths and the risk of being four-bet. Calling allows me to realize my equity and use my positional advantage post-flop.

The flop is relatively dry, and I am holding Ace-high with no pair. Being out of position in the small blind, checking allows me to evaluate the opponent's reaction to the board. I have two overcards, which gives me some equity, but committing more chips at this stage without a made hand is unnecessary. Checking is a prudent move to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

On a board of Jack-Five-Four, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Flashlight, who raised pre-flop from the small blind, has checked to me. While I could bet for protection against overcards or draws, checking back is a more prudent play with a marginal hand like bottom pair. This allows me to control the size of the pot, keep their range wide, and potentially induce a bluff on the turn while avoiding a check-raise that would force me off my equity.

The board has become quite draw-heavy with the arrival of the Seven of Spades on the turn, completing potential straight draws and flush draws. I hold the Ace of Spades, giving me the nut flush draw and two overcards. Since the flop went check-check, my opponent's range is likely wide, containing many marginal hands that will struggle to call a bet on this turn. By betting, I can apply pressure, potentially take down the pot immediately, or set up a strong bluff if I hit my flush on the river. A bet of 300 represents strength and capitalizes on my high equity.

On the turn, the board is 4s-5d-Js-7s. I have bottom pair (fours) with an Ace kicker. After the flop checked through, Flashlight leads out for 300 into a 400 pot. In a blind-versus-blind battle, bottom pair is often worth a call against a delayed continuation bet, as the opponent's range can include many semi-bluffs, overcards, or missed draws. I am getting roughly 2.3:1 on a call and have sufficient equity to see the river.

On the river, the board (4s-5d-Js-7s-7h) has paired the seven, and there is no completed flush or straight. My hand, Ace-high, has no showdown value. My opponent called my turn bet, suggesting they have at least a piece of the board (likely a Jack, a Five, or potentially a pair of 4s/7s). Since I cannot beat any hand that would call a value bet, and checking behind gives me a chance to see a showdown for free if my opponent also has a weak hand, checking is the most prudent action to avoid losing additional chips.

On the river, the board has paired sevens, making my hand two pair (sevens and fours) with an ace kicker. After Flashlight led into me on the turn and checked the river, it's unlikely a bet from me will extract value from weaker hands, as most hands that would call a bet here (such as a Jack, a Five, or a pocket pair like 66) already beat my fours. Furthermore, my hand has decent showdown value against missed straight draws (like 68) or missed spade flushes. By checking back, I ensure I reach showdown and realize my equity without risking a check-raise or bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Holding 8-6 offsuit, a very weak hand with poor equity, it is not profitable to call 100 chips to enter the pot. Even in position on the button, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve stack size.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, Ace-high is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately or take control of the hand out of position.

T4o is a very weak starting hand. Even though I have position against the Small Blind post-flop, facing a 3x raise pre-flop with this hand is a clear fold. Defending with such weak offsuit cards will lose chips in the long run.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit in a 3-handed game. Ace-high hands are generally ahead of the random ranges of the blinds and constitute a standard opening range from this position. I have the chip lead and want to maintain aggression. A standard 3x raise is consistent with my previous play style in this tournament.
I am dealt J8o in the Small Blind facing a Button raise. This hand is too weak and disconnected to defend with out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

Queen-Ten suited is a strong holding in the Big Blind, offering good playability and equity against a button open. Calling allows me to control the pot size while seeing a flop with a hand that has good potential for straights and flushes, as well as top-pair strength.

I have trip Queens with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand on a Q-Q-K board. Checking to the pre-flop raiser (Flash) is standard from the Big Blind as it allows them to continuation bet with their entire range, including bluffs and weaker made hands. This keeps the pot growing when I have a strong hand and helps disguise my strength.

The flop of Qc-Qs-Kd is very heavy and favors the range of the Big Blind caller who could easily hold a King or a Queen. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have Ace-high, which holds some showdown value but is vulnerable to a check-raise on this specific texture. By checking back, I control the pot size and allow myself to potentially realize equity on the turn or catch a bluff later, rather than turning my hand into a bluff on a board that is difficult to represent successfully if the opponent has any piece of it.

I have three Queens with a King kicker (trips) on a board of Qc Qs Kd 3s. Flash checked the flop, which could mean they have a King, a weaker pair, or they are just floating. Since the board is relatively dry, I want to start building a pot to get value from any King or middle pair Flash might have. Leading out with a bet of 350, which is slightly more than half the pot, is a solid value bet to force a call from weaker hands.

On a board texture of Qc-Qs-Kd, Flashlight leading out for 350 after I checked back the flop indicates they either have a made hand (a King, a Queen, or a small pair) or are attempting to take the pot away after I showed weakness. My Ace-high has very little equity against even a small pair, and I only have three relatively clean outs to an Ace. Given that the board is very heavy and I checked the flop, my range is capped, making it difficult to continue without a piece of the board. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better opportunity.

Queen-Ten offsuit is a very playable hand from the Button. A standard 2.5x raise allows me to put pressure on the blinds to either steal the pot outright or take control of the hand going into the flop with positional advantage.

My hand, 9-6 offsuit, is extremely weak and lacks potential. Being in the small blind, I am forced to play out of position against the button (Pro) for the remainder of the hand, and the big blind (Flash) is still to act behind me. Calling here would be a mistake, as the risk of losing chips in a poor position with a marginal holding outweighs any potential reward. The disciplined play is to fold and conserve my stack.

I am holding pocket sixes in the Big Blind against a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Pro). Pocket sixes is a strong medium pair that plays well in a head-up pot. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot down immediately, calling is a solid play that allows me to set-mine with good implied odds given our deep stacks (80+ BBs). Being out of position post-flop, I prefer to keep the pot controlled for now.

I have a middle pair (pocket sixes) on a relatively dry, 9-high board (3d 2h 9s). As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise from the Button, standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand is strong enough to call most continuation bets, but leading out (donk betting) here doesn't accomplish much against the Button's wide range and gives away the strength of my hand. I will check and see how the Button reacts.

On this very dry and disconnected 9-3-2 rainbow board, my opponent's range is unlikely to have connected strongly. As the pre-flop aggressor with position, a standard continuation bet of around half the pot puts pressure on my opponent to fold their unpaired overcards and weak broadway hands, allowing me to take down the pot uncontested much of the time.

I have pocket sixes on a 9-high board (3d 2h 9s). This is a strong middle pair given the dry texture of the flop. The Button (Pro) has made a standard continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 550. While my hand is likely ahead of much of Pro's wide button opening range (which includes many unpaired overcards), raising here might force out the very bluffs I want him to continue with. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see a turn card, where I can re-evaluate based on whether an overcard (A, K, Q, J, T) appears. Folding is out of the question as my hand has significant showdown value on this specific board.

The board has paired on the turn with a Trey of Hearts (3h), making the board 3d-2h-9s-3h. My pocket sixes are still a solid middle pair, but they remain vulnerable to any Nine or higher pocket pair Pro might be holding. Given that Pro was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, standard play is to check to him again to see if he continues his aggression. Leading out now doesn't provide much information and might fold out hands I currently beat while getting called or raised by hands that have me beat. I will check and evaluate his response.

The turn pairs the 3, which doesn't improve my Queen-high and is unlikely to have improved my opponent unless they floated with a 3. Since they check-called the flop, their range heavily features a 9, medium pocket pairs like 44-88, or a stubborn Ace-high. Firing a second barrel as a bluff might fold out some floats, but it is risky against an opponent who has already shown interest. Checking back controls the pot, gives me a free card to potentially hit a Queen or a Ten, and avoids unnecessarily bloating the pot with a weak hand.

The river Jack of Spades is an overcard to my pocket sixes and potentially hits the Button's range (hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, JT). Given that Pro checked back the turn, he likely has a marginal hand with some showdown value or a total air hand. By checking, I allow him to check back with his Ace-highs or smaller pairs that I beat, or to attempt a bluff with missed overcards. Betting myself wouldn't likely get called by any hand worse than mine, so checking to reach showdown is the most profitable and risk-averse play here.

The river brings the Jack of Spades. My opponent called the flop and checked both the turn and the river, indicating a range of marginal made hands such as a 9, small pocket pairs like 44-88, Ace-high, or a Jack that just improved. With Queen-high, I have virtually no showdown value and could only win the pot by bluffing. However, attempting to bluff now would require a substantial sizing to fold out a stubbornly played pair or Ace-high, while risking a call from a Jack or a 9. Given the low success probability of a bluff and the lack of strong draw completions to represent, giving up and checking behind is the most conservative and stack-preserving play.

K3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand from the button in a three-handed game. Given the poor equity of this hand and the lack of a favorable position to justify playing it, the most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a stronger holding.

My pocket fives have zero showdown value on a board of 2d-7c-4d-Jd-Qh. Pro's river bet of 1800 into a pot of 3600 indicates strength, such as a queen or jack, or a missed draw turned into a bluff. This heavy action suggests a strong range, likely AK or QQ+, but pocket Jacks are too strong to fold at this stage of the tournament. With the pot offering decent odds and being in position, I will call the 2200 more to see a flop and re-evaluate, keeping my stack manageable rather than overcommitting in a potentially dominated situation.

I have AdKd, a premium hand that is extremely strong in a 3-handed game. Pre-flop, Flashlight (Button) opened, I 3-bet to 1050 for value, and Pro (Big Blind) 4-bet to 2500. Flashlight then cold-called the 2500. This created a large pot of 6050 chips. My current commitment is 1050, and I need to call 1450 more to see a flop. However, given the low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) that would result (approximately 1.0) and the fact that I am out of position against both players, calling is less optimal than raising. By shoving all-in, I maximize fold equity against hands like JJ, TT, or potentially even QQ, and I ensure that I realize my full equity by seeing all five cards. AKs blocks AA and KK, making it less likely I'm dominated. Given the aggressive history of both Pro and Flashlight, AKs is comfortably ahead of or flipping against their overall ranges. Pushing now also simplifies the hand and avoids a difficult post-flop scenario 3-way.

Facing a 5-bet jam from the Small Blind and a cold-call of my 4-bet from the Button, AKo loses a lot of its appeal. The Button's cold-call of a 4-bet is extremely strong, often indicating a slow-played monster like AA or KK. The Small Blind's 5-bet shove further narrows their range to premium pairs or AK. In a multi-way all-in pot against these ranges, AKo is frequently dominated or flipping at best, making it a negatively expected value call. With 74.5 big blinds remaining and only 7 hands left in the tournament, preserving my stack is the most prudent decision.

I am in a situation where I have already committed 2,500 chips into a very large pot. With my pocket Jacks and only 4,325 chips remaining in my stack, I am pot-committed. Even if the small blind's 5-bet suggests a range of QQ+ or AK, the pot odds are far too favorable to fold. At this late stage in the tournament (Hand 143/150), folding would be an overly conservative move that likely loses me the tournament anyway. I must call the remainder of my stack to see the showdown.

With only 14 big blinds remaining and 6 hands left in the tournament, I need to find a double-up opportunity. However, 7-4 suited is a very weak hand to start that process, even from the button. Pro and Flashlight have significant stacks compared to mine, and shoving here would be over-betting the pot with low equity, while a small raise would commit too much of my stack to a hand that likely won't survive a call. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening in the final hands.

With A4o in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong enough hand to attack the big blind. I will make my standard 2.5x open raise to pressure the big blind, hoping to steal the pot pre-flop while retaining equity if called.

I am in the big blind with A-10 suited, a very strong hand for heads-up play against a small blind raise. Pro has been active, and calling the 150 to see a flop is a solid, standard approach. It keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to utilize my position and hand strength post-flop.

With Ace-four offsuit on a Ten-Nine-Five flop, I have completely missed the board and have only Ace-high with no strong draws. This flop connects fairly well with the Big Blind's calling range, as hands like Ten-X, Nine-X, and straight draws are very possible. Acting out of position, a continuation bet is unlikely to get many better hands to fold and could get me into trouble if called or raised. Checking to control the size of the pot and evaluate my opponent's action is the safest play.

I hold top pair (Ten) with an Ace kicker on a fairly dry board (Th, 5d, 9s). Pro checked, indicating potential weakness or a desire to induce a bluff. As the aggressor in this situation, I should bet to gain value from middle pairs or lower-ranking hands. A bet of 250 into the 500 pot is a standard sizing to maintain pressure.

I completely missed the flop with A4o, and the Ten-Nine-Five board connects very well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws). Facing a bet, I have no pair, no draw, and limited equity. There's no reason to invest more chips in this hand from out of position.

I am on the Button with A8o, which is a standard steal/opening hand in this position. Flash is short-stacked in the Big Blind with 1300 (13 BBs) and Flashlight is deep in the Small Blind. A standard raise to 250 puts pressure on the blinds to fold, and A8o has decent equity if we get shoved on by the short stack or called by the SB.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Five suited, which is a marginal hand. With the tournament nearing its conclusion and my stack size significantly larger than the button (Pro), there is no need to play speculative hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am the short stack with only 13 big blinds (1300 chips) and there are only 6 hands remaining in the tournament. King-Jack offsuit is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly against a button open-raise. While I could call the 150 to see a flop, my stack size makes a stop-and-go or a check-fold post-flop very costly. By shoving all-in now, I maximize my fold equity against Pro's opening range and ensure that if I am called, I am realizing the full equity of my hand. Given the tournament stage and my stack relative to the others, this is a standard 'shove or fold' spot, and KJo is well within the shoving range.

Flash is extremely short-stacked after a massive loss in hand #143. His 3-bet to 1300 out of the big blind with only 100 chips behind is effectively an all-in shove. With A8o on the Button, I have a strong enough hand to call a 14BB shove against a desperate short stack's wide range. I will raise to 1400 to put him officially all-in and avoid any post-flop action for his remaining chips.

I have 100 chips remaining and I am being asked to call 100 more into a pot of 2750. This gives me pot odds of 27.5 to 1. Regardless of what Pro is holding, King-Jack offsuit has more than enough equity to justify calling for the last of my chips. At this stage of the tournament, with only a few hands left and a very short stack, I must commit to the hand and hope to double up.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, 3c-2h. Given that I have a significant chip lead of 21,300 against the Pro's 8,550, there is no need to risk chips on a sub-par hand. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament.

97 offsuit is a marginal hand even in a heads-up scenario. With 86 big blinds and only a few hands left in the tournament, there is no need to commit chips or attempt a steal with a weak holding. Folding is the safest play to preserve the stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the button/small blind position with a strong Ace-high hand (A8o). Given my significant chip lead over the opponent, I want to take the initiative and exert pressure pre-flop. Raising is the standard play to capitalize on the positional advantage and the strength of my hand.
I am in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand, 8s 6c. Flashlight raised to 250 from the Button/SB. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
94o is a very weak hand, well below the standard opening range even from the Button in heads-up play. With no significant equity and 85 BBs remaining, folding to save chips is the optimal decision.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With a large stack advantage of 21,450 against the opponent's 8,400, I should play aggressively. Facing a 3x raise from the button in heads-up play, pocket fives are certainly strong enough to defend. Calling allows me to see a flop, looking to hit a set or play back on a low-card board, without bloating the pot out of position against a loose range.

With an underpair to the 9 and 7 on a somewhat coordinated board (7s 2s 9d), checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard line. This allows me to gauge Flashlight's strength and control the size of the pot out of position. I can evaluate his continuation bet sizing to decide if my pocket fives are worth a call.

The board is 7s-2s-9d and I hold Ad-Kh. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have two overcards and the opponent has checked to me. This board is relatively dry and favors my range. A continuation bet is a standard play to represent strength and potentially take down the pot immediately. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot, to pressure my opponent and capitalize on my position.

With pocket fives on a 9-7-2 board with a flush draw, I am facing a half-pot continuation bet out of position. I only have third pair and no relevant backdoor draws. Calling here is highly marginal because many turn cards (spades or overcards) will force me to fold to a second barrel anyway. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a difficult out-of-position situation on later streets in this final hand.
